BMW Price different between Electronic car and Petrol Cars by region.

Author

Jason Chen

Introduction

BMW is a major vehicle compony throughout the world, they sell both electric and petrol vehicles in the global markets. There are many aspects lead to different regional pricing strategies like tariffs, consumer preferences, subsidies and market structure. This research will examine whether the price differences between BMW electric and petrol vehicles vary significantly across regions. Furthermore, this project will investigate whether these pricing differences are influenced by the historical sales performance. I believe that by doing this we can understand BMW’s pricing logic and provide practical insights for dealers and market analysts in the context of the global automotive market during this energy transition period.

The dataset used in the research is BMW sales data (2010-2024) dataset form Kaggle. This dataset includes the detail of every car BMW sold from 2010 to 2024. The detailed information include model, year, region, color, fuel type, transmission, engine size, mileage, price in USD, sales volume, and a sales classification variable. By using this dataset, with my skill on data wrangling, data virtualization and regression modeling, the research is aiming to understand BMW pricing logic, and how regional difference influence on global market pricing.

Ethical Considerations

I think the risks of this dataset and project related to privacy or individual identification are really small, because this dataset only contains non-personal, product-level data such as model, region, and price. But I still recognize that the process of collecting data naturally contains some biases. Different regions may have varying standards of data reporting, data cleaning, or pricing documentation. So, the results from the data may show something unexpected. To remain ethically responsible, all the outcomes and illustrations in the project should be presented strictly with the evidence of the data. In the project, I shouldn’t exaggerate or ignore any data outcome in order to provide a better result.

Data Explanation and Exploration

This dataset contains detail information of every car BMW sold from 2010 to 2024, including Model, Year, Region, Fuel_Type, Engine_Size_L, Mileage_KM, Price_USD, Sales_Volume, and Sales_Classification. Each row represents one BMW model sold in a specific year and region. The key variables for this project are Price_USD, Region, Fuel_Type, Year, and Sales_Volume, which are essential for analyzing regional pricing strategies and potential adjustments based on past sales performance.

One challenge in this dataset is the large number of distinct values across different columns. For example, there are 11 different BMW models and four fuel types. This complexity means that, during analysis, I need to focus on the main research question and carefully select which variables to control, in order to avoid results being influenced by too many confounding factors and keep the code and result clear.

Code 
library(dplyr)

Attaching package: 'dplyr'
The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag
The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
Code 
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(kableExtra)

Attaching package: 'kableExtra'
The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':

    group_rows
Code 
df <- read.csv("BMW_sales_data (2010-2024).csv")

region_count <- df %>%
  group_by(Region) %>%
  summarise(Count = n())

ggplot(region_count, aes(x = Region, y = Count))+
  geom_bar(stat = "identity",fill = "#66c2a5")+
  geom_text(aes(label = Count), vjust = -0.5)+
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, max(region_count$Count) + 1000))+
  labs(
       title = "Number of BMW Listings by Region",
       x = "Region",
       y = "Number of Listings")+
  theme_minimal()

This plot shows how many sales BMW made across different region in this dataset in total. The result presents that each region has a similar number of observations, ranging from approximately 8,200 to 8,450, which indicates that the dataset is relatively balanced. This is important to know becuase we can compare prices across regions directly without worrying that a region with more data will dominate the analysis.

Code 
df <- df %>%
  mutate(Fuel_Category = ifelse(Fuel_Type == "Electric", "Electric", "Fuel Car"))

fuel_distribution <- df %>%
  group_by(Region, Fuel_Category) %>%
  summarise(Count = n()) %>%
  ungroup()
`summarise()` has grouped output by 'Region'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.
Code 
ggplot(fuel_distribution, aes(x = Region, y = Count, fill = Fuel_Category))+
  geom_bar(stat = "identity")+
  geom_text(aes(label = Count), position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3)+
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Electric vs Fuel BMW Models by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Number of Listings",
    fill = "Fuel Category"
  )+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Electric" = "#66c2a5", "Fuel Car" = "#fc8d62"))

This chart shows the distribution of BMW models by fuel type in different rigion. From the chart we can see that Fuel Car dominate about 75% of the market in across all region.This indicate that consumers around the world still prefer BMW’s traditional fuel car more than electric car. Moverover, the proportion of fuel cars to electric vehicles is similar across all regions, indicating that BMW maintains a relatively consistent product mix worldwide.

Code 
ggplot(df, aes(x = Fuel_Category, y = Price_USD, fill = Fuel_Category)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~Region, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Price Distribution of BMW by Fuel Category Across Regions",
    x = "Fuel Category",
    y = "Price (USD)",
    fill = "Fuel Category"
  )+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Fuel Car" = "#fc8d62", "Electric" = "#66c2a5"))+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

The boxplot shows the price distribution of BMW models by fuel category (Fuel Car vs Electric) across six regions. As the plots show, the price ranges for electric and fuel vehicles are nearly same within each region. Furthermore, the overall distribution of fuel cars and electric vehicles is consistent across regions, indicating that BMW maintains a similar product mix worldwide. This suggests that regional market does not significantly influence BMW’s pricing strategy, and the proportion of electric to fuel vehicles remains stable regardless of location. So that we can say that at least in this scope of data, BMW pricing strategy is globally approach rather than region specific approach.

Statistical Analysis and Interpretation

[ Provide at least two distinct versions of your statistical model. Report your results so that they appear as a polished table.]

Code 
model1 <- lm(Price_USD ~ Year + Fuel_Category, data = df)
summary(model1)

Call:
lm(formula = Price_USD ~ Year + Fuel_Category, data = df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-45340 -22617    -48  22588  45174 

Coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)           32214.95   54230.50   0.594    0.552
Year                     21.35      26.89   0.794    0.427
Fuel_CategoryFuel Car  -322.18     268.72  -1.199    0.231

Residual standard error: 26000 on 49997 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  4.134e-05, Adjusted R-squared:  1.334e-06 
F-statistic: 1.033 on 2 and 49997 DF,  p-value: 0.3558
Code 
coeffs <- summary(model1)$coefficients
model1_table <- data.frame(
  Term = rownames(coeffs),
  Estimate = coeffs[,1],
  Std_Error = coeffs[,2],
  t_value = coeffs[,3],
  p_value = coeffs[,4]
)

print(model1_table)
                                       Term   Estimate   Std_Error    t_value
(Intercept)                     (Intercept) 32214.9537 54230.49994  0.5940376
Year                                   Year    21.3491    26.88633  0.7940505
Fuel_CategoryFuel Car Fuel_CategoryFuel Car  -322.1818   268.71677 -1.1989643
                        p_value
(Intercept)           0.5524897
Year                  0.4271698
Fuel_CategoryFuel Car 0.2305475

This regression model examines how BMW prices are affected by Year and Fuel Category. The intercept 32,215 USD represents the estimated base price. The coefficient for Year (21.35) suggests that every year the average prices of BMW increase about $21on, but this is not statistically significant becuase the p-value is 0.427, indicating that year-to-year changes are minor. The coefficient for Fuel Car is -322.18 USD with p = 0.231, meaning fuel cars are on average slightly cheaper than electric vehicles, but the difference is not statistically significant.

Overall, this result shows that BMW’s pricing does not vary strongly over time or between electric and fuel vehicles in this dataset, supporting that BMW maintains a globally consistent pricing strategy rather than a region or fuel specific strategy.

Code 
features <- df %>% select(Price_USD, Engine_Size_L, Mileage_KM, Year)
fuel <- df$Fuel_Category

features_scaled <- scale(features)

pcaModel <- prcomp(features_scaled)
pcaDF <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pcaModel$x[,1],
  PC2 = pcaModel$x[,2],
  Fuel_Category = fuel,
  Price_USD = df$Price_USD
)
pca_summary <- summary(pcaModel)$importance
print(pca_summary)
                           PC1     PC2       PC3       PC4
Standard deviation     1.00548 1.00135 0.9990203 0.9941153
Proportion of Variance 0.25275 0.25068 0.2495100 0.2470700
Cumulative Proportion  0.25275 0.50342 0.7529300 1.0000000

This PCA model examines how the main features of BMW vehicles, Engine Size, Mileage, Year, and Price, relate to each other and whether they separate electric and fuel cars.From the result we can see that proportion of variance for each factor is around 25% (PC1 = 25.3%, PC2 = 25.1%, PC3 = 24.9%, PC4 = 24.7%), indicating that no single combination of features dominate the price.This result suggests that BMW’s pricing strategy does not rely on individual vehicle characteristics. Overall, with the regression analysis from Model 1, it supports the conclusion that BMW maintains a relatively consistent, globally uniform pricing strategy, with only minor differences between electric and fuel vehicles.

[ Provide at least three polished visuals that specifically support and validate the model(s) you have developed (e.g., residual and regression line/scatter, histogram showing normality of data or residuals, etc.), or help to communicate your main result. Visuals should have captions and be referred to clearly in your text, and they should not all be the same (e.g., not three scatter plots). ]

Code 
df_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(Year, Fuel_Category) %>%
  summarise(Mean_Price = mean(Price_USD), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(df_summary, aes(x = Year, y = Mean_Price, color = Fuel_Category))+
  geom_point(size = 3)+
  geom_line(size = 1.2)+
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#66c2a5", "#fc8d62"))+
  labs(
    title = "Average BMW Price per Year by Fuel Category",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Price (USD)",
    color = "Fuel Category"
  )+
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.

This data visualization presents average BMW vehicle prices per year from 2010 to 2024, separated by Fuel Category. In the graph we can see that from 2010 to 2019 electric vehicle average prices steadily increased, and reach the peak at 2019, and then gradually declined. In contrast, fuel vehicle prices have remained relatively stable around $75,000. Overall, electric vehicles tend to have slightly higher prices than fuel cars, but the difference is not large.Combined with previous analyses, The small premium for electric vehicles may reflected by the production costs or consumer preferences, but it does not indicate a major deviation in BMW’s overall pricing approach.

Code 
ggplot(pcaDF, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Fuel_Category))+
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA of BMW Features Colored by Fuel Category",
    x = "PC 1",
    y = "PC2",
    color = "Fuel Category"
  )+
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Fuel Car" = "#fc8d62", "Electric" = "#66c2a5"))+
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

This plot shows BMW vehicles distribution of the first two principal components. From the plot, we can see that Electric and Fuel Cars are mostly overlap, meaning there is no clear separation. This suggests that these features do not strongly determine the difference in pricing between Electric and Fuel Cars.

Code 
df <- df %>%
  mutate(Predicted_Price = predict(model1, newdata = df))

df_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(Year, Fuel_Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Actual_Mean = mean(Price_USD),
    Predicted_Mean = mean(Predicted_Price),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(df_summary, aes(x = Year))+
  geom_point(aes(y = Actual_Mean, color = Fuel_Category), size = 3)+
  geom_line(aes(y = Predicted_Mean, color = Fuel_Category), linetype = "dashed", size = 1)+
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Electric" = "#66c2a5", "Fuel Car" = "#fc8d62"))+
  labs(
    title = "Actual vs Predicted Average BMW Prices by Fuel Category",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Price (USD)",
    color = "Fuel Category"
  )+
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

This plot shows actual average BMW prices per year (points) versus predicted prices from Model 1 (dashed lines). The points is same with Figure 4. The dashed lines show the model’s predictions, which slightly increase over time, likely due to technological improvements and inflation. The predicted prices also confirm that electric vehicles are consistently higher priced than fuel cars, support the finding from Figure 4 again.

Conclusions

Based on the analysis of BMW sales data (2010–2024), it is clear that BMW employs a relatively consistent pricing strategy across both fuel types and regions. The PCA analysis indicates that BMW does not base its pricing on any single feature of the vehicles. This approach is highly suitable for a large company like BMW, which offers a wide variety of products and engages in a global market.

The regression analysis shows that BMW prices gradually increase each year, and electric cars tend to be priced higher than fuel cars. Based on previous findings, these price differences are likely more related to improvements in vehicle quality and the effects of inflation. Additionally, the fact that electric vehicle production is still in its early stages, growing consumer preference for electric cars, and supportive policies promoting electric vehicles may all contribute to the higher prices of electric cars.

Furthermore, the automotive industry is highly complex and influenced by local policies, government subsidies, tariffs, and other global factors, such as oil prices. All of these factors can greatly affect vehicle sales. In this context, the results of this research can be use as strong evidence that BMW employs a consistent, universal pricing strategy. Which aligns with the global marketing standardization perspective discussed by Zou and Cavusgil (2002). As Zou and Cavusgil note, “the standardization perspective emphasizes how much a firm’s product, promotion, channel structure, and price are standardized across the country markets,” highlighting that multinational firms often seek consistent pricing across regions.

However, to fully understand the automotive market or to predict future prices, it would be necessary to collect more detailed and larger-scale data on regional promotions, dealer networks, and local demand.

Project Reflection}

In this project, I used a BMW sales dataset from Kaggle. Since the dataset was already clean and most the variables is clear. So the data cleaning and wrangling process did not take me much time. One of my concern from begining was if the number of data from each region is different, it could introduce bias if one region dominate the dataset. So my first visalization compared how many sales BMW made across different region in this dataset in total. The result is that the counts of data from different region is mostly similar. Which reassured me that no single region would disproportionately influence the results.

The most effective plot I created in this project would be my Visual 3, a boxplot showing the price distribution of BMW models by fuel category across six regions. From this plot I concluded that bmw is using a consistent pricing strategy across both region and fuel type. All the models and visualiaztions that followed were designed to provide more evidence to support this conclusion. And I found it really effective becuase with one visuliazation I presents the price distribution across six regions.

During this project, one rule I was following the whole time is: always stay aligned with my research question. I wrote the topic on a sticky note and sticked it on my computer. Every after time I finished one plot or finished one interpretation, I revisited the note and check whether what I got were still conected to my topic and help a better understanding for my project. This kept my visualiaztions and interpretations on track. Becuase from the previoous expriences, I know that when working on a large project with multiple components, it is very easy to get lost of topic and unintentionally shift away from the original goal. This strategy helped prevent that from happening.

One fun thing in this project was choosing the colors for my plots. In past labs, I felt my color palettes were either too harsh or not attractive enough. Last week, by coincidence, I was watching a Miami Dolphins game highlight on YouTube, and I realized that the blue and orange in their uniforms were both eye catching and comfortable to read. Inspired by that combination, I decided to use those colors in my plots for this project. For me, the visual presentation in this project turned out very satisfying.

At the beginning of the project, I noticed that the plot I created showed a strong pattern of consistency across regions and fuel types. This was very different from what I originally expected. I had predicted that there would be large regional differences, and that electric and fuel-powered cars would follow completely different pricing strategies. I initially hoped to discover a big differences then I could provid rich business analysis. Because of this, I was disappointed with my early plots and even questioned whether there were mistakes in the data or in my visualizations. However, as I continued creating additional plots, each one keep supporting the idea of a unified pricing strategy. This gradually convinced me that BMW truly follows a standardized approach. I also began to understand that such consistency is reasonable and beneficial, as it helps maintain a strong and coherent brand image across global markets.

Works Cited

Eshummalik. (2024). BMW sales dataset (2010–2024) [Data set]. Kaggle. https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/eshummalik/bmw-sales-dataset?resource=download

Zou, S., & Cavusgil, S. T. (2002). The GMS: A broad conceptualization of global marketing strategy. Journal of Marketing, 66(4), 40–56.
